title: Attitudes to Muslims in New Zealand
contents:
Contents
Background
Prejudice trends 2012-2020 reveal “Muslim Acceptance Gap”
Acceptance of Muslims increased after the attacks
However Muslim acceptance lags behind other groups
Suggestion: 51 seconds of silence
Appendix: background







Background
New Zealand ranks among the world’s most tolerant societies (OECD 2011).

The NZAVS measures tolerance using validated “affective thermometers.”
However, Muslims are New Zealand’s least accepted minority group (Shaver et al. 2016) [Shaver et al. (2017); Hawi et al. (2019); Yogeeswaran et al. (2019); (Greaves et al. 2020; Sibley et al. 2020; Highland et al. 2019; Hawi et al. 2019).(See Fig 1)
The tragic 2019 Christchurch mosque attacks raised awareness of Muslim adversity, and were followed by gains in Muslim acceptance. This gave Muslims hope.
Yet acceptance changed at different rates within New Zealanders, and among a sizeable minority, prejudice remained entrenched.
Here, we using New Zealand Attitudes and Values Study data, we quantify the magnitude of the anti-Muslim prejudice in New Zealand before and after the 15 March attacks.

The New Zealand Attitudes and Values Study (NZAVS) is a pioneering nationally representative panel study started in 2009 (2021), which has measured over 65,000 New Zealanders.
Prejudice trends 2012-2020 reveal “Muslim Acceptance Gap”



Figure 1: Within-person prejudice change pre/post attacks towards (a) Muslims, (b) Immigrants, (c) Māori, (d) NZ Europeans. Redline indicates mosque attacks. Greenline indicates maximum warmth to Muslims, which was strongly boosted post-attacks, yet lags behind other groups



Acceptance of Muslims increased after the attacks



Figure 2: (fig:pptimeline) There was a substantial lift in Muslim Acceptance following the shootings, however there maximum average level of Muslim acceptance remains lower than all other minority group ratings since the NZAVS has been recording attitudes within New Zealanders.






However Muslim acceptance lags behind other groups






Figure 3:  Increase in Warmth to Muslims After Shootings, however, the acceptance of Muslims lags behind other minority groups. Green horizonal line indicates peak acceptance of Muslims, which occurred following the attacks, and has since dropped.









Suggestion: 51 seconds of silence

Democracies must cultivate virtues of respectful listening.
The civic virtues necessary to sustain democracies should not be assumed. Such virtues must be actively cultivated.
Respectful listening in a culturally diverse democracy requires education. That is, to understand how people think we must know something about them.
With these principles in mind, we suggest an annual moment of silence on March 15 for freedom of religious expression, to acknowledge the losses, and to open a space for conversation.
Appendix: background
In the context of New Zealand history, the relatively high levels of prejudice against Muslim, as well as the confusion of Muslims with Arabs, might seem surprising. First, New Zealand’s cultural diversity exceeds that of Australia, the United Kingdom, France, Germany, the Netherlands, and Scandinavia. Muslims are only a small part of this diversity. In 2018, New Zealand’s Muslim, just over 1.5% of the total population. The Islamic community has been present in New Zealand since at least 1874 (Shepard, 2006). The New Zealand Muslim community is of diverse ethnic and cultural origins — of those that affiliated with Islam in 2013, 26.9% were born in Asia, 25.7% in New Zealand, 23.3% in the Middle East or Africa, and 21% in the Pacific Islands (Center, 2013). Thus, fewer than a quarter of New Zealand Muslims can be classified as ‘Arab.’

Colonial New Zealand state never adopted an official state religion, while principles of religious equality and the promotion of social harmony led to the formation of a secular state primary education system beginning in 1877. In such a setting where religious conflicts are few, anti-religious prejudice of any kind is anomalous.
Another reason that anti-Muslim in New Zealand is puzzling is that New Zealand is one of the world’s most socially progressive and tolerant societies (OECD 2011), which has a long tradition of religious tolerance and accommodation of religious diversity.
A final source of puzzlement about anti-Muslim prejudice in New Zealand is that there is no record of any extremist Muslim group in New Zealand, nor have there been any reported attacks of Muslims on non-Muslims By contrast many attacks against the Muslim minority were reported prior to 15 March 2019, when 51 Muslims were murdered at two mosques in a single day. Members of Islamic communities in New Zealand have long reported verbal and physical harassment, vandalism, negative media portrayal, and/or have faced difficulties in the labour market (Shepard, 2012). Thus, while Muslims in New Zealand have a basis for concern about their experiences of anti-Muslim prejudice, there is simply no basis for anti-Muslim prejudice among the non-Muslim majority is in the interpersonal experiences of non-Muslims.



2021. https://www.psych.auckland.ac.nz/en/about/new-zealand-attitudes-and-values-study.html.


Greaves, Lara M, Aarif Rasheed, Stephanie D’Souza, Nichola Shackleton, Luke D Oldfield, Chris G Sibley, Barry Milne, and Joseph Bulbulia. 2020. “Comparative Study of Attitudes to Religious Groups in New Zealand Reveals Muslim-Specific Prejudice.” Kōtuitui: New Zealand Journal of Social Sciences Online 15 (2): 260–79.


Hawi, Diala, Danny Osborne, Joseph Bulbulia, and Chris G Sibley. 2019. “Terrorism Anxiety and Attitudes Toward Muslims.” New Zealand Journal of Psychology (Online) 48 (1): 80–89.


Highland, Benjamin R, Geoffrey Troughton, John Shaver, Justin L Barrett, Chris G Sibley, and Joseph Bulbulia. 2019. “Attitudes to Religion Predict Warmth for Muslims in New Zealand.” New Zealand Journal of Psychology (Online) 48 (1): 122–32.


OECD. 2011. “Society at a Glance: OECD Social Indicators.” OECD.


Shaver, John H, Chris G Sibley, Danny Osborne, and Joseph Bulbulia. 2017. “News Exposure Predicts Anti-Muslim Prejudice.” PloS One 12 (3): e0174606.


Shaver, John H, Geoffrey Troughton, Chris G Sibley, and Joseph A Bulbulia. 2016. “Religion and the Unmaking of Prejudice Toward Muslims: Evidence from a Large National Sample.” PLoS One 11 (3): e0150209.


Sibley, Chris G, M Usman Afzali, Nicole Satherley, Anastasia Ejova, Samantha Stronge, Kumar Yogeeswaran, Michael Grimshaw, et al. 2020. “Prejudice Toward Muslims in New Zealand: Insights from the New Zealand Attitudes and Values Study.” New Zealand Journal of Psychology 49 (1).


Yogeeswaran, Kumar, M Usman Afzali, Nadia P Andrews, Elizabeth A Chivers, Meng-Jie Wang, Thierry Devos, and Chris G Sibley. 2019. “Exploring New Zealand National Identity and Its Importance for Attitudes Toward Muslims and Support for Diversity.” New Zealand Journal of Psychology (Online) 48 (1): 29–35.
